C[Rathbone: seems like black is going to get a huge corner, could white play at o15 or m16 instead, given that the side group is strong enough?

Uberdude: Yes, this is good for black, but once you've got this far white really ought to extend. A standard resistance is to probe at r17 right after black q14. If blach hanes on outside at q17 then white can follow the game line, as s16 remains as a big yose. If black hanes on inside (as I will if r17 comes later as in game) then p13 can hane at p14 and when black cuts it's an even fight. Also q14 can be answered at s15.

C[betterlife: Hi Graham, i try to make some common comments about playing on 13x13 boards. And point the few mistakes i saw in your game.
C[betterlife: Hi Graham, i try to make some common comments about playing on 13x13 boards. And point the few mistakes i saw in your game.

C[betterlife: We both play on the 3-4 point(komoku). The 3-3 (san-san) is also possible.
Rather desatvangeous is hoshi 4-4 because hoshi is influence orientated. Influence usualy has a big range of it's extension (there where the influence goes too) and the board 13x13 is just too small for that (usualy an oponent's stone in the other corner already erases a lot of influence)

C[betterlife: I almost play always here. To protect the corner and fast realize some points looks important to me. The other reason is that l4 in the other corner squeezes a litte black's kakari at l9. Black will have a hard time to settle on the right side.

C[betterlife: An other good reason to play this small shimari is that the next move for white is sente. If white plays j3, black answers h3 then white has to defend at k3 and ends up gote. On the small board it is _very_ important to keep sente.

C[betterlife: Unluckily there is no fuseki theory for 13x13 boards. All i can tell about my strategies is that i played a lot of 13x13 games on itsyourturn.com against a strong 5 Dan (Calvin) and could win some of them. I could never beat him on the 19x19 board.

C[betterlife: If white plays l6 it is like an overplay there because the right bottom corner is already kind of safe and the  black l9 stone is already kind of in a squeeze (ok i am exagerating a lot now but it is the direction of thinking).

C[betterlife: This move is the first failure of black. Should be at e3. Black should use the two j3/j4 to push white against it not to make territory with it. If white tenuki than it is a good move but white won't tenuki he will for sure seperate left bottom corner from black bottom middle group. Correct play are at d3, e3 or d6, c6.
C[betterlife: White attacks blacks bottom group and starts setteling him self.
C[betterlife: Usualy extend to g3. Now if you play like this it is very agressive or very risky or simply wrong. Let's see what happens next.

C[betterlife: White makes better shape but also a very slow move. He protects the only weakiness that balck h3 created. I think this is typical in Go: If the opponent plays a complete stupid and bad move the other player is so stunned about it that he thinks ""This move looks so bad that it might be good?!"" and plays the direct answer instead of punishing it or ignoring it.
Might be that to punish correctly a wrong move is also really difficult and sometimes dangerous too.

C[betterlife: H11 is a ladder breaker. But if black plays c3 he can get the corner and or a least reduce it. This is a complex and difficult situation here. There is no other solution but to think this whole thing through. Usualy make some overall analysis and choose the 3 or 4 possible moves and really try to think them through. b4 would not be in this possible move selection because it is too small (for territory) and too defensive. I remember having played some games against Calvin on itsyourturn.com and i thought about every move around half an hour to find some good answers in close games. If he would only spend some seconds to answer? I don't know it!!!

C[betterlife: Gote and the end. Black needs 4 stones to kill one white stone and gains 2 points. White plays two stones in the corner and and gets 8 points and comes out with sente. Makes 6 points for white   2 points possible territory lost for black. Makes around an 8 point win in this corner for white and sente is worth around 8 point so white wins only here in this corner 16 points and takes a clear lead.
